Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Pmq Deposition Testimony And Document Production At Deposition
Off Calendar Jul-03-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. Blake Bostwick, Esq. of Bay Area Bicylce Law (tel: 415-466-8717) for the Plaintiff.
Atty. Osmaan Khan, Esq. of Gordon Rees Scully Mansukhani (tel: 510-463-8600) for Defendant Rad Power Bikes, Inc.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF ANTHONY REYNA's Motion To Compel Pmq Deposition Testimony And Document Production At Deposition.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion to compel PMQ deposition testimony and document production is granted in part and denied in part.

There is a liberal policy in favor of discovery and inquiry may be made into "any matter, not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter... if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence." (CCP 2017.010.) The PMQ discovery will reveal defendant's official corporate response (after investigation if necessary) and is different from the other percipient witnesses' testimony that was based on personal knowledge. (See Maldonado v. Superior Ct. (2002) 94 Cal.App.4th 1390, 1395.)

Balanced against this are Plaintiff's delay in seeking PMQ discovery, the breadth of topics and documents sought, and the fact that expert discovery has concluded. The court generally grants some relief but narrows the topics in light of that balance.

The court refers the parties to Exhibit F of the Khan Declaration.

For the topics listed in Exhibit F as "Internal", the court orders Defendant to produce a person most qualified on the topics listed in Exhibit F as Plaintiff's compromise offer as to Nos. 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, 11. It denies relief as to Nos. 5, 7, 10.

For the topics listed in Exhibit F as "Customer service," the court orders Defendant to produce a PMQ on the topics listed as Plaintiff's compromise offer as Nos. 1, 2, 3. It denies relief as to Nos. 4, 5, 6.

For the topics listed in Exhibit F as "Design," the court orders Defendant to produce a PMQ on the topics listed in Plaintiff's compromise offer as Nos. 1, 2 3, 4. The court denies relief as to Nos. 5, 6, 7.

For the topics listed in Exhibit F as "Incident," the court orders Defendant to produce a PMQ on the topics listed in Plaintiff's compromise offer as Nos. 1, 2, 3, 10. The court denies relief as to Nos. 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11.

Plaintiff also moves to compel production of numerous categories of documents. Plaintiff has not provided a separate statement for its document production requests or set out specific facts showing good cause for production pursuant to CCP 2025.450(b)(1). In any event the burdens of production at this date are substantial.

The depositions shall occur by July 16, 2025 unless the parties agree otherwise.

The court denies sanctions, finding Defendant's opposition substantially justified.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling, with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. da Fonseca; Court reporter: Sharon Cahn, CSR# 6210 (tel: 714-813-0320 / email: heysharon9@aol.com). (301/CVA)

MOTION FOR LEAVE TO File The Proposed First Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Jul-03-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. Blake Bostwick, Esq. of Bay Area Bicylce Law (tel: 415-466-8717) for the Plaintiff.
Atty. Osmaan Khan, Esq. of Gordon Rees Scully Mansukhani (tel: 510-463-8600) for Defendant Rad Power Bikes, Inc.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF ANTHONY REYNA's MOTION FOR LEAVE TO File The Proposed First Amended Complaint.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion to file the First Amended Complaint is denied without prejudice in light of the current trial date of July 21, 2025.

A court may deny amendment where there is undue delay and prejudice to the opposing party. (Roemer v. Retail Credit Company (1975) 44 Cal.App.3d 926, 939-40 ["even if a good amendment is proposed in proper form, unwarranted delay in presenting it may-of itself-be a valid reason for denial."]; Magpali v. Farmers Group, Inc. (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 471, 488 ["Where the trial date is set, the jury is about to be impaneled, counsel, the parties, the trial court, and the witnesses have blocked the time, and the only way to avoid prejudice to the opposing party is to continue the trial date to allow further discovery, refusal of leave to amend cannot be an abuse of discretion."].)

Here, plaintiff alleges that the basis to add the proposed claim for fraud/concealment and punitive damages was discovered in February 2025 and was "crystallized" by subsequent expert examination. (Bostwick Decl., par. 3.) Plaintiff, however, waited until June of 2025 to file this motion. Plaintiff's delay was unreasonable. The court notes that despite plaintiff's representation, the operative original complaint does not seek punitive damages. The proposed amendments change the tenor of the case to one of fraud seeking punitive damages. Defendant has no opportunity to conduct discovery regarding these new claims or file dispositive motions based on the current trial date. The court therefore concludes that allowing the amendments would be prejudicial. If the trial date is continued, plaintiff can renew this motion.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling, with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. da Fonseca; Court reporter: Sharon Cahn, CSR# 6210 (tel: 714-813-0320 / email: heysharon9@aol.com). (301/CVA)
